name: Willis Tower
fact: The nine bundled tubes let it be the world's tallest building for 25 years — you can still see each tube stop at a different height.
description: Formerly Sears Tower, Willis Tower defined Chicago's skyline from 1973 to 1998 as the tallest building in the world. SOM's Fazlur Khan invented the bundled-tube structural system here to make the enormous height economical.

name: St. Regis Chicago
fact: The tallest building in the world designed by a woman-led firm; its stepped 'stems' were tuned by wind-tunnel testing to shed vortices.

name: 875 North Michigan Avenue
fact: The Xs on its facade aren't decoration — they are the building's structure, carrying wind loads to the corners.
description: Known for decades as the John Hancock Center, 875 N Michigan was SOM's first tall trussed-tube. The exposed X-bracing lets it be tall and slender with less steel than a conventional frame.

name: Trump International Hotel and Tower
fact: Its three setbacks line up with the heights of Wrigley, the river, and Marina City — a deliberate rhyme with the skyline.

name: Aon Center
fact: Its original Carrara marble facade began cracking and falling off; the entire skin was replaced with granite in the early 90s.

name: Aqua
fact: The wavy balconies aren't just sculptural — each one is shaped to give specific residents a view past their neighbors.

name: Marina City
fact: The corncob towers were a bet against 1960s suburban flight — a self-contained city with parking spiraling up the first 19 floors.

name: AT&T Corporate Center
fact: The pinkish granite crown was originally lit gold at night — a deliberate 1980s response to Aon's cold white marble.

name: Two Prudential Plaza
fact: The chevron spire is a direct homage to the Chrysler Building — a rare Chicago wink at New York.

name: 311 South Wacker Drive
fact: The illuminated crown was designed as a lantern for the skyline — locals just call it 'the tower with the glowing hat.'

name: IBM Plaza (330 North Wabash)
fact: Mies died before it opened; the black glass tower on the river was his final skyscraper.

name: Chicago Board of Trade Building
fact: The faceless aluminum figure on top is Ceres, goddess of grain — the sculptor left her featureless because no building would ever look her in the face.

name: Tribune Tower
fact: Embedded in its walls are stones from the Great Pyramid, the Great Wall, the Alamo, and 150 other world landmarks — Tribune correspondents smuggled them home.

name: Wrigley Building
fact: Its terracotta gets whiter as it rises — six shades, so it seems to glow against the sky. It's floodlit at night year-round.

name: Chase Tower
fact: Its sweeping A-frame curve was born of a zoning constraint: banks needed massive floor plates below, offices needed slender ones above.

name: 150 North Riverside
fact: It sits on a footprint the size of a house — a slender concrete stem widens above the tracks it straddles.

name: Kluczynski Federal Building
fact: Mies' last Chicago work — the black steel and glass grid that became the template for every corporate tower after.

name: Carbide & Carbon Building
fact: Legend says the black-and-gold facade was inspired by a champagne bottle — the top is the foil, the tower is the neck.

name: Merchandise Mart
fact: It was the largest building in the world by floor area — big enough to have its own ZIP code until 2008.

name: Chicago Temple Building
fact: The spire houses an actual chapel 400 feet up — the highest sanctuary in the world when it was built.

name: Jewelers' Building
fact: The core hid a car elevator so jewelers could drive their inventory straight up to their offices, safe from stickup men.

name: Auditorium Building
fact: The theater inside has some of the finest acoustics ever built — Adler tuned it before microphones existed.

name: James R. Thompson Center
fact: The pink-and-blue glass drum was so hot and loud that state workers famously hated it — Google is now restoring it as its Midwest HQ.

name: Sullivan Center (Carson Pirie Scott)
fact: Look at the ornament framing the corner entrance — Sullivan's iron filigree at eye level is why he's remembered.

name: The Rookery
fact: Frank Lloyd Wright remodeled the light court in 1905 — the gold-leaf staircase inside is the reason to go in.

name: Mather Tower
fact: The upper floors taper so aggressively the top three stories share a single office each.

name: Fisher Building
fact: Look for the actual fish carved into the terra cotta — a rebus on the developer's name, Lucius Fisher.

name: Manhattan Building
fact: The world's first 16-story building with a full skeleton steel frame — the moment vertical cities became possible.

name: Monadnock Building
fact: The last tall building built with load-bearing masonry — its walls are 6 feet thick at street level to hold up the ones above.

name: Reliance Building
fact: Its terra cotta and glass facade is the direct ancestor of every glass skyscraper built since.

name: Navy Pier Centennial Wheel
fact: A landmark by shape, not height — visible from most of the lakefront and often mistaken for a bigger building at distance.

name: Harold Washington Library
fact: The seven-story owls perched on the roof are almost as big as the library's reading rooms below.

name: Jessie White State of Illinois Building
George W. Dunne Cook County Administration Building
Everett McKinley Dirksen United States Courthouse
Hilton Garden Inn Chicago / North Loop
Embassy Suites by Hilton Chicago Downtown Magnificent Mile
Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago
